commentBody: Newspaper version pdf is not working for second consecutive day. Shows only error message.
Super fast puzzle today but fun.

Cute puzzle and a nice double debut, but we already had a Monday puzzle this week. The constructors are definitely not BAD APPLES or SPOILED BRATS, and I'm not throwing ROTTEN TOMATOES, but the editors seem to be OFF COURSE.

(Is an emu really a FUNKY CHICKEN?)

Not my cup of tea at all.

No magician has ever said TA-DA! The puzzle went downhill from there with the clues for SHAM, PAST, ALLY, NEST,and many others.

Finally, the OFF COURSE had next to nothing to do with the theme answers (unless we twist ourselves into pretzels).

Congratulations on a quality debut!
Loved this theme set—tight, funny and a great revealer.
The clue for PAPERCUT is absolutely first rate. I thought there were a lot of fresh entries, actually, for a Tuesday. Great job.
Early post calling it a Monday puzzle is a good barometer of how the comments will go: We'll get some quite unkind responses to those who express the opposite opinion.
Had a moment when I thought Curiosity & Perseverance must have been the names of 2 ancient Greek LOVERS 🤦‍♀️

I can't applaud the meal that missed the mark, but I can and do applaud the constructors on their debut. Congrats and thank you, Gus and Bharati! (Here's Mazzy Star with "FADE INTO You" - https://youtu.be/ImKY6TZEyrI?is=579bAKyOsJVdZdxb)

I've lived in MN for all 43 years of my life. Not once did I ever consider a loon call to sound like a laugh.

Google tells me it is. Huh. TIL I guess...

I'm having a rash of "Not Quite" messages appear of late.  I think for every puzzle in the last five days other than Monday I was utterly stumped by a cross.

I had no alternative to running the alphabet on the BOBA/BUSTA cross.

Eww, send this puzzle to the compost pile!
(Referring to the theme, that is!)

Now wondering if there is a way to compost human BAD APPLES.  Mix them together with other scraps, maybe some manure, let them heat up, and rest…end up with a nourishing useful final product?

I thought it was fun! Maybe a bit way, I did beat my average for a Tuesday, but hey, if we can have extra hard days then it’s not a big deal to also have some slightly easier ones. I don’t think we need to demand like Goldilocks that our puzzle porridge be just right every single time. That’d get exhausting. I thought some of the clues were fresh and cute. I liked it, overall.

Childish.  Yes, even for a Tuesday.  The theme was pretty cute though.  I'm going back into seclusion again until there are some signs of challenge 'round these parts.  Editors, I know the dollars are the driver, but there is little fun here anymore.  :(

To those who like TV Guide level stuff and despise us whiners, well have at it, and enjoy yourselves.

I'm not getting the "Monday puzzle" complaints. I've only been doing the NYT crosswords for a few decades but can barely discern the difference between the Monday and Tuesday levels of difficulty.

Wednesdays are a definite and perceptible step up.
Very fun Tuesday, my favorite was Funky Chicken. And congrats on the debut!
Gosh, if these puzzles seem too easy, and you want more of a challenge… maybe use solely the down clues, or solely the across clues, or solely the even, or odd numbered clues.

I was firmly committed to MArs instead of PAST at 8 Across, which threw me OFF COURSE. But I'm always up for a food theme, so congrats to the constructors on their debut. Today's song is suggested by 47 Across. Here's David Johansson with FUNKY But CHIC live on Conan. (Bonus points for a leaping Conan.) https://youtu.be/EcqckTknXoo?si=oIzCtcJ7s9c-3xSw

Note for Bharati, if you're reading this.
Cryptic fan here. (I used to sneak off to the University library to photocopy the /real/ Times crossword.) Where might we find your cryptics? Under your  own name or did you use a nom de croisée?

Alternative clues:

FUNKY CHICKEN = foul fowl
ROTTEN TOMATOES = gross grocery
SPOILED BRATS = uncouth youth
Has anyone else noticed of late the phenomenon of some posts allowing for recommending or replying to and others not?

That was pretty tricky. I was OFFCOURSE for quite a while, having INREVERSE instead. 
Also, not really sure what SNOOT is, I was pretty certain I had it right as SNOUT and TOPKNOTS was not familiar enough to me to help

Nice debut Gus and Bharati!  The revealer was perfect.  

SPOILED BRATS was my favorite because the BRATS are not immediately recognized as food, but I can't imagine that there are many (any?) other entries that would work that way.
Seriously good Tuesday puzzle. Lots of amusing clues. Well done!

Fantastic Tuesday.

Solid de-beaut!

Felt the cryptic vibe running through the clues!

Felt like a Tuesday...but I was also off balance the whole time. Which is a good thing! Shake off smugness and expectations!

Awesome.
Would have been lovely if LAME DUCK or COLD TURKEY could have been thrown in, but there's only so much room and I have no SOUR GRAPES.

Amused to see a FRAPPE clued as [Chilled coffee drink].

Back when I was growing up in the Boston suburbs in the 1980s (and well before then), “frappe” was the New England term for an ice cream milkshake (a “milkshake” literally just being milk and flavored syrup, without the ice cream).

In the early 1990s, the Boston-area coffee chain Coffee Connection introduced a blended iced coffee drink called a “Frappuccino” — a portmanteau of “frappe” and “cappuccino” — which became a big hit. 

When Starbucks started looking to expand into the Boston area, the company decided to simply buy Coffee Connection and take over its stores. A large part of the reason was to get rights to the (trademarked) Frappuccino. (In a recent episode of the “Acquired” podcast, you can hear Starbucks founder Howard Schultz purist’s disdain for the sweetened, milky, barely-any-coffee dessert-disguised-as-a-beverage concoction that of course went on to make Starbucks gobs of money). 

So cluing FRAPPE as [Chilled coffee drink] is a curious kind of backward proprietary eponym. 

Maybe I will have a tamale or some paninis with that frappe.

There is an issue with your clues for STAT and ASAP. 
It is a really simple distinction that drives me crazy. 
As Soon As Possible means just that. Do this thing as soon as you possibly can. Finish what you are doing and then do this thing, put this thing next on your list, etc.
STAT means stop what you are doing NOW and do this instead.
When the clue is I need this immediately, the answer is STAT.

Fun and clever theme with a revealer that perfectly lands.

Kudos to the brain that noticed that there are a handful of phrases combining a word meaning “bad” and a food, then crafted this theme.  l, who love the quirks of our language, absolutely adored it.

If anybody’s solve went quicker than usual, a contributing factor may have been that the box has one less row than usual, thus 15 fewer squares to fill in.

All the theme answers had pop, and there was a lovely supporting cast, with SOME NERVE, PAPER CUT, FRAPPE, and TOP KNOTS.

Your puzzle, Gus and Bharati, infused me with a quick boom of happy.  You’re my top bananas, and congratulations to both of you on your NYT debut!

Masterful work by the editorial team, hanging onto this puzzle so it could appear in conjunction with the nasty outbreak of cyclosporiasis that's now affecting much of the USA. 

OK, I'm feeling queasy---not sure this puzzle passes the breakfast test.

(Congrats on the debut!)

They're not actually rotten, but definitely getting off-ish. Not recommended for use in the fruit bread.

EXPIRY DATES

@Matt (Spring Hill)

This didn’t show up in the thread, so I’m trying sending it as a new post:

It used to be $250 for a daily and $500 for a Sunday, but they recently raised the amount a little, and I’m not sure of the exact amounts. 

They also instituted a small premium after the tenth (I think) acceptance, so some people think that’s why we don’t see our old favorites as much anymore.

TIL: Halal.
Got the theme entries early on, choked at the revealer.

Some very clever misdirects! Great Tuesday puzzle!
This was a really good puzzle – great start to a very hot and humid Tuesday. Thanks Gus and Bharati.
Congrats on the debut. It was a really fun Tuesday.

Bears aren't just a Rockies thing--even east of the Mississippi, the Eastern Black bear, while less dangerous, can be a problem. But I've found raccoons to be a more persistent nuisance.

I don't think of crêpes folded, so much as rolled. But you can also stack them, layer them with a filling--savory or sweet (just make sure your butter isn't rancid)--then slice them like a gâteau; save a slice for the bear. Happy Bastille Day, everyone!
The clue for PAPERCUT was worth the price of admission!
Posted a related comment as a reply but it needs more. I am surprised to be the first to complain about the inaccuracy of 59D under current management.

Congratulations to both of you on a fantastic debut!! I thought the theme was very clever. After living in Wisconsin for four years and eating plenty of BRATS in that time, that answer really got a laugh out of me once I got to the revealer! 🤣 I’m looking forward to the collab coming out near Halloween and hope to see more from both of you!!

Nice Tuesday puzzle. Typical slow start for me, but then just had a good time working things out from the crosses. Looking forward to more from our new constructors, and...
...suspect that we may never see 'Bloxham', 'Bharati' or 'Hemmady' as answers in a puzzle. 

:) 

And I'll put my puzzle find in a reply. 

....
Fun one today. Had to guess a couple of spots but they came through correct and got the gold star. Happy Tuesday

I got as far as BAD APPLES and immediately thought of my own puzzle of 3/10/21.  Would the revealer be the same?

When I got to 21A, I realized that the revealer would not be the same.  So what would the revealer be?

One by one, the possibility of having a revealer with BAD, SPOILED or ROTTEN in it was eliminated. So was FUNKY -- though that one never crossed my mind.

Had all the synonyms been eliminated?  What about turning everything the other way around?  I thought of possibilities like PAST ITS PRIME; NO DARN GOOD; TRASHY FARE.

OFF COURSE is a cute revealer that I never thought of -- made much more interesting by turning BRATS into hot dog food.  Also, there was a Friday-worthy clue for PAPER CUT.  I liked this amusing Tuesday puzzle a lot.

A lot of space could be saved here in the comments circus if the column began each day with a poll:

Today’s puzzle felt like a

Monday
Tuesday
Wednesday
Friday
Saturday
Huge Mistake
Dream

It included (check all that apply)

A Natick
Arcane trivia 
A word from a foreign language that scared me
A word that, when spelled backwards, is OERO

Technologically speaking, the platform failed me in the following ways (list)

Lastly, the editors have ruined the venerable NYT Crossword by bowing to pressure from: (limit response to one institution or demonic figure; avoid proper nouns)

The first two themers I happened to solve were BADAPPLES and ROTTENTOMATOES, so naturally I thought the revealer was going to be something like FRUITOFTHELOOM.  FUNKYCHICKEN dissuaded me from that theory, and SPOILEDBRATS got me even further off course.  (Great puzzle, Gus and Bharati!)
This one took longer than the usual Tuesday for me because I had SNOUT instead of SNOOT for a long time. TUPKNOTS didn’t sound right but what do I know about sumo hairstyles?
Dazzling double debut dudes! To see some TOPKNOTS in action the July Grand Sumo Tournament ISON. https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/shows/sumo/

Despite the theme, this puzzle felt fresh and new. And fun.

Thanks, Gus and Bharati! More, please.
Lots of great entries here! I especially liked DOX and the cluing for PAPER CUT. Thanks for a fun, fresh feeling frolic.

Loved the Theme!! Like others had a little difficulty with the SW. Had SNOUT but that didn’t work with the Sumo’s… 

Rating:
8/10
Fun puzzle!
Today, I knew the rapper's name without a single cross. I didn't see many of the down clues, actually, so I missed "Lachrymal unit" until afterwards.

Not sure what happened to my post (or why) but I don't really have time to revisit ...  I got distracted looking at the FAB parade celebrating Bastille Day (makes Macy's T'giving Day and the Rose Bowl both look so inadequate--though I always enjoy the latter.)  Wow.
This is also our son's birthday--at last! At last!--  Sadly, this year I forgot to send a "Happy Due Date" card on July 1st.  I have a photo in an album--in which I am smiling because I think I only have three more weeks to go before our new baby will arrive! Ha ha.  10 lbs. 5 oz.

Oh, the puzzle.  Hmm, well 21A suggests getting rid of BRATS, which of course does cross one's mind when witnessing  a badly-behaved youngster causing an uproar or spoiling an event, but the puzzle doesn't actually cite how to dispose of them...

Really a bit unappetizing at the breakfast table...Even the best of gardeners will see some blossom-end rot, or find an apple spoiled by a codling moth--(they'll ruin the whole crop if you don't spray)--but only the most careless of shoppers/cooks would let BRATwurst or CHICKEN go bad.  I guess Just put all your ROTTEN food in one puzzle and toss it!

I was caught by BRATS too.
A nice crossword - HEFTY is fairly unusual, and theme clues were good fun.

Is there really an International Speak Like a Pirate Day?  
There seem to be quite a few celebration days in the US (Teacher's Day, Secretaries Day I know of) which must be a welcome diversion. The UK has a few national 'Bank Holidays' - such a dull way to celebrate, with no parades or fiestas.
Had no problem with BRATS ... since growing up in Wisconsin, brats were always a staple at a barbeque,

I seem to remember that someone was claiming recently, when  BRAT appeared in the puzzle, that "nobody has ever called a BRATwurst a BRAT" or words to that effect.

I wonder where that mistaken person is today?

[Lachrymal unit] is a great clue! For some reason, it reminded me of this scene, from Jeunet and Caro's *City of Lost Children* (1994). I'm not sure how much sense it will make out of context, especially since the scene of Miette actually producing the tear is cut off from the beginning of the clip, but the description in YouTube might help: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKCjRHhjVhE

One more puzzle - a Sunday from February 16, 1992 by Joel D. LaFargue with the title "Crypt-Teaser." Don't think I would have had much of a chance with this one. Anyway - some theme clue and answer examples: "I AM A CAD MA" MACADAMIANUTS "STONE'S DAD" TOSSEDANDTURNED "MACARONI" ROCKYMARCIANO "UNSET" LOONEYTUNES "DRAW A BOY" OFFBROADWAY And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=2/16/1992&h=47d .....

I thought I was a sci-fi time traveler to the PAST yesterday when a national fruit basket company's website told me to fax them my company's tax exempt form...  Fax!? 🤯🤯🤯🤯 Granted, I've been struggling to track days and months lately, but I thought I knew the year! Fax!?!?

So I did what any reasonable person would do and built a time machine... I managed to successfully fax my form and moved on with life—which I decided would be best respent in 1987. It really was a good year! Got my can of Aqua Net, will time travel!!  Har!

Okay, I know some will say that faxing is not obsolete, especially in medical and legal fields, but I literally haven't had to fax since 2013—not in my work or personal life. 

Well, the real tripper was today I checked in with them to confirm the fax arrived. They said they don't know what that fax number is and that I should email it to them...  🤯🤯🤯🤯🤣

All you can do is laugh. I spent valuable time figuring out where and how to fax something in the year 2026 and they just say, oh, you should have emailed. To which I say, hey, you should update your website before my grapes get more sour... 

Ah well, not the fault of those I was talking to, so I didn't become a SPOILEDBRAT, but hopefully they'll suggest an update to the site. 

Thank you, NYT, so much, for bringing up such a painful subject. While you're at it, why don't you give me a nice PAPERCUT and pour lemon juice on it?! Har!! 😏 

Enjoyed the puzzle though! Fun one!!  Mmmm, brats!!

What a COaRSE puzzle! I held my SNOOT the whole way through.  (Just kidding.)  Congratulations on a fine debut.

Kudos, for some TOPKNOTch cluing!  Thought I ws going to BUSTA gut laughing when I finally got 8D.

I miss Life magazine. I was a subscriber to the print edition and loved the photography.

On the plus side, it's impossible to get a 8D from a digital publication.
The crossword is  now available at 8 P instead of 7P Monday through Friday. Is this time permanent?
Sigh.  Everyone seems to think this was easy.  Not me.  3+ minutes over my Tuesday average.  Different strokes for different folks.

poem made from words in today’s puzzle

a/ we little we
we dance in the woods
chirp and look
we word-speakers we
we listen dear companion
listen to the d/ dead 
the he’s and the she’s 
whisper in pearls 
need everything 
gradually
immediately
beloved
everything 
when all of this life
a/ fades 
into 
ohio
a classic in my book 5 stars it's funny witty literate bravo!!!!

Every once in a while I get reminded that valuable storage space in my brain is getting taken up by something that's of almost no use. Today that happened to be knowing the exact date of International Talk Like a Pirate Day. It wasn't even very helpful in this puzzle since I figured out one of the crosses ASAP.
Just here to say I was away on vacation with (purposely) no internet. I am SO glad I went back and did Weds., July 1 (the one with the mirror NE and NW corners). What a cool puzzle!

Nice puzzle, but very easy! 
It helped that I filled in BADAPPLES and ROTTENTOMATOES on my first pass with no crosses. 
5:44 may be a new Tuesday PR.

Fun one. congrats.

I found myself thinking about LOONs in the lyrics of the Great American Songbook. There are two off the top of my head:

Wanna cry, wanna croon
Wanna laugh like a LOON
It's the old devil moon in your eyes.

and

Crazy as a LOON
Sad as a gypsy serenading the moon.

can anyone think of any others?
It's showing error message now for me too, but at 10 pm on the dot when I clicked on the Newspaper Version it presented the Standard Version.

@Barry Ancona
Wondering why you always print the Newspaper Version instead of the .pdf? For me, it uses too much toner, and I have no need to see yesterday's solution. Only do it when the NYT makes it the default or I can tell that there's something about the puzzle that will work much better with that format.

Times Rita,
I like to see the puzzle exactly as it appears in the paper.

@Barry Ancona

You realize knocking the editors is as bad as knocking the constructor, don't you? They're all human beings.

@Elbridge Gerry

It's not remotely comparable.

Setters are not reimbursed for their labor; setting isn't a job.  It's an avocation.  

The difficulty level lies not with them but with the editors and the latter are paid to put out a final, appropriate package.

Lately the editors have been a huge disappointment in the execution of their job.

Finally, your remark seems to suggest that no one's work should ever be criticized because it's wrong to say something less than flattering about any human being.

Agreed re:  constructors. 

But here our criticisms tend to say much more about the commenters’ own esthetics than about the quality of the constructions. 

You castigating a Kugelman or me swooning over a Weintraub are comments essentially about ourselves. 

The grids themselves are nearly always fine exemplars of the wonts of their setters. 

Kugelman has his adherents. just as Weintraub has her detractors.

@Matt

There is that, yes, but also, sub-par construction is a thing. Having a zillion three-letter entries, for example, because that's the only way to make some spanners work, will always seem like a cheap trick, to me. Or introducing naticks, especially multiple ones, on crossings that work OK phonetically and linguistically with a choice of letters.

@Matt

Once I was defeated by the crossing of an actress called MARL_ and a guy named D_ERR.

You'd think a woman would be called MARLA, right? That's a totally reasonably thing to assume, anywyz if you don't know her, specifically. And given the name Kotb exists, DAERR looks perfectly fine.

Well, the actress's name was MARLO...

I don't believe in god, but I swear by naticks existing.

@Andrzej

MARLO Thomas.  Danny Thomas's daughter.

Duh!

See?  No natick.

@Matt

Yes, I can throw around random names you've never heard about, too.

@Andrzej

Bet you can't.

And you take it back.

That Girl was the furthest thing from random.

She and Ginger from Gilligan's Island were two of my most giving imaginary girlfriends when I was a wee teen.

@Matt

One correction: NYT constructors are paid for their work. Not enough for most of them to make it their full-time job, but they don’t work for free, either. 

Still, your point stands. The constructor submits a puzzle, and the editor selects the day and adjusts some, if not many, clues to fit that day. 

The other thing not mentioned is that the editor may not have free rein in making decisions about difficulty levels. But we have eleven through that before in these comments.
N.B. See the median solve times: https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-07-14 https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-07-13

@Andrzej

I was just about to mention her so you'd have a frame of reference.
@Barry Ancona yes, but it is also about having fun with the clues

@Matt

And as you did so, you also contemplated Proverbs 12:22, eh?

Was "Cute puzzle and a nice double debut..." not clear?
I enjoyed the puzzle. My constructive criticism was that it should have been slotted for a Monday.
@Barry Ancona Lewis pointed out that the grid is 15 boxes smaller today.
Yes, he did. And I think that's another good reason for this one to have run on a Monday.

@Andrzej

When don't I?

@Matt

When do you?
@Dan. Off course had everything to do with the theme answers.  Course is part of a meal
@Dan Those clues were all perfectly fine, and the theme makes sense.

@Dan
  You've never taken a bite that tasted "off"?
@Matt https://youtu.be/X6qqks-TfJM?is=AVR9fP7MoVL0EV6D
@Francis You were complaining literally last week that your posts were deleted because someone was targeting you.

@Matt

You might notice that if someone says they found the puzzle difficult or had trouble with a certain crossing, people usually offer helpful advice, or say nothing.

The snarky responses are almost exclusively directed toward unkind posts.  Not the "I had trouble with this one." type of comments but the "This constructor doesn't know how to make a puzzle and should find another hobby." type of comments.

I may have missed it, but I've never seen you call out the original comment, only the response.

@Nancy J. I've said before that the condescending comments by a certain group of people are always in response to someone saying the puzzle was too difficult.

When someone posts that the puzzle was too easy (meaning these people agree with the poster), they don't get that vitriol.

It has the effect of gatekeeping, even though they claim, "We're just trying to help." 

As someone else posted here a few days ago, "Shaming people in the guise of educating them is always narcissism."
@Nancy J. I've shown examples in posts on other days where this is not true.

@Nancy J. Agreeing with Matt. 

The constant gaslighting about this from people like you, Nancy, is tiring. We know what’s happening.

@Matt


I'll be on the lookout.  I'll never hesitate to admit when I'm wrong.

@Nancy J.

I'm with you Nancy. 

I know full well he's is talking about me, and probably Andrzej, along with a lot of the old guard (old is a compliment here, not a pejorative).

I think I've seen the "examples" that are spoken of.  I was not impressed.

But this is a battle we'll never win.  The perfect example of what he's talking about is something he's already pointed out (without any links) or something that's about to be commented.

Bring the receipts, man.  Let's see your examples with actual links, and let's hash them out.

Of course, *this* post itself will be submitted as evidence.  When similar evidence was presented before the response was "I think you just proved my point."

@SBK in TO

It's just a three of four second clip of the Simpsons of a couple of yahoos getting into a fight.

@Kristin

Those would be great names for teenagers caught up in the love and the hormones.

@Kristin
To me they read as two daughters in a pioneer or Amish family.
@Kristin i had the same with RIVERS 😸

@Kristin

I briefly considered the two lions at the NY Public Library.
@Steve L Oh, love that thought!

@Kristin

They are, correctly, Patience and Fortitude, of course.

@Jacqueline

I've never really thought it sounded like a laugh either, but on the other hand I'd be hard pressed to say what it *does* sound like.

It's such a strange, eerie, lonely call.  

I like to set up my telescope by an inland lake on the North Shore which has about as dark skies as are available east of the Mississippi.  

One night I was set up and doing some observing and a loon on the nearby lake gave a call.  I was just transformed by the perfection of the moment, seeing a globular cluster, hearing a loon.

I don't know that life on earth gets much better than that.

@Francis
What a serene image that paints, Francis. I will help bring on a dreamy slumber. Cheers!
There are four different calls. The eerie one Francis mentions is different from the crazy laugh one. I’d give you my demo of the laugh call - well, you’ll just have to imagine it.

@Francis. I oohed and awed when I saw my first globular cluster.  Like a bee swarm of sparkly diamonds.  With a loon call… heavenly.

Only time I saw the aurora was up in Ely MN.  Delightful memory.  Used to work at the Soudan mine.

@sonnel

Yeah.  A year or so ago we had the best nights for viewing, and watching that faint glow resolve into so many individual sparkles....

The (New Hampshire) Loon Preservation Committee has samples of the four types of loon calls on their website: https://loon.org/the-call-of-the-loon/ The fourth, which they call the tremolo, is the "laugh". I've vacationed on Squam Lake in NH for 30 years or so and recognized [Lake bird with a laughing call] immediately - and which call it was referencing.

@Cat Lady Margaret For many years, there was a series of short films on Canadian TV Called "Hinterland Who's Who". They provided brief portraits of Canada's flora and fauna. Their website today happens to feature a Banana Slug. 😄 But among their very first features in the 1960s – in black and white! – was the loon. Go to https://www.hww.ca/wildlife/birds/loons/ Listen to their calls, wander around the website, and enjoy.

@CRTH
See, /this/ kind of post is why I hang around here.

@CatsPaws

And cheers back at you!
This thread is loony tunes.

@Jacqueline
I too thought it odd, but I went down the rabbit hole and listened to several recordings. Loon calls are much more varied than I'd realized, and one of them does indeed sound like laughing.

@Francis Always so interesting to see where my mileage is different from others—those were two of my gimmes. Boba tea is a favorite of my daughter’s. Usually my downfall is anything involving TV or movies, although I’m slowly learning from doing these puzzles. Today’s solve was relatively smooth except for falling for the scam/sham misdirect that Sam called out. If I hadn’t looked back over my crosses, I would have been stuck with a “not quite” cALAL meal.

@Francis

Strangely enough, those two were gimmes, for me 😲

@Andrzej
For me, too, even though I had BOBA tea exactly once and would never think to listen to BUSTA.
@Francis I'm gonna be in Grand Marais today. I'll cruise through town blaring Busta Rhymes for you. Lyrics very NSFW: https://youtu.be/un3NkWnHl9Q?si=N5fc4bIi_dSpa-g0

@Cat Lady Margaret
“Soylent Green is people!”

@BobSolo,

Oh, man … how ‘bout a spoiler alert next time? 😄

@NYC Traveler,
Oops!  My bad 🤣
@Carla a bit easy *
@Carla Absolutely! The incessant demands that puzzles meet some unwritten, subjective standards of difficulty are really annoying.
@B One word: archive

@B
Gosh, because it's all about you. 

Perhaps there are MANY newcomers to the Times Crossword who appreciate being able to learn as they go and appreciate the gradual ramping of difficulty. 

There's a hundred years of Friday's and Saturday's in the Archive if you are so 'brilliant'.

@Paul V Also btw the plural of Friday etc. is Fridays, Friday’s is a possessive,as in “Friday’s puzzle was so easy I went to sleep doing it.”

Apostrophes as random accessories for “s” (ESS in crosswordese) are something I can fuss about and be distracted from more important manifestations of the collapse of civilization.
@VN I can almost always finish Monday in less than 10 minutes but Tuesday takes me longer.

@VN

I know it's a Monday when puzzle when I can almost finish it using just one set of clues (across or down). No way today fit that description. I think the Too Easy Crowd is getting a little carried away.
@sonnel Seconded! I just did it using only the across clues in 18:34. Excellent puzzle, not at all SPOILED by a FUNKY theme!
@Vaer oh, that was a great performance!

@SBK in TO

I think most people don't use STAT at all. It strikes me as specifically hospital jargon. But perhaps its usage has bled over from TV shows.

@Eubanks Not so sure ASAP also means "Finish what you're doing and then do this thing."  If you were halfway through a crossword puzzle and asked to help put out a grease fire in the kitchen ASAP, would you take that to mean that the grease fire could wait until you finished the puzzle?

@Eubanks
surface dive suggests that "stat" is derived from the Latin "statim" meaning "at once". My guess is that in medical environments it has that specific meaning, but as it spread into the general language it acquired a more general meaning.

@Bill

We pulled into Grand Marais a couple of years ago in October, I think.  Coming in the door, the we heard a rustle in the apple tree just off our front deck, and we saw a dark figure.

Turned out it was a black bear, a smallish one, enjoying one gigantic bite out of each apple, the rest being discarded.

People visiting Grand Marais gathered in the street taking pictures.

Sadly, though, the theory was that a drought at a critical time in the summer had reduced the berries available, and the bears were putting up with humans to try to get ready for winter.  I'll always wonder if the little guy made it.
